reasoning: The informal statement assumes the framework of a "concrete category (C, U) over B", which by universal mathematical convention (nLab, Joy of Cats, Mac Lane, Riehl) definitionally requires U to be faithful. The Lean formalization correctly surfaces this implicit premise by adding `CategoryTheory.Functor.Faithful U`. The other hypotheses match exactly: `HasLimitsOfSize.{v, v} C` matches "C has all small limits", `PreservesLimitsOfSize.{v, v} U` matches "U preserves them", and the bi-conditional conclusion `ReflectsLimitsOfSize.{v, v} U ↔ U.ReflectsIsomorphisms` matches "U reflects small limits iff U reflects isomorphisms". The `.{v, v}` universe annotations encode "small" limits in the standard Mathlib way. This is a textbook case of surfacing an implicit premise (the faithfulness of U in the definition of a concrete category) that the informal statement relies on but does not spell out in the theorem line itself.
